title: Japanese milk bread
subtitle: Pillowy soft white bread loaf using the tangzhong method
excerpt: Japanese milk bread (shokupan) is the softest white bread you can make at home. The secret is tangzhong: a small portion of flour and milk cooked together into a paste before mixing into the dough. This pre-gelatinised starch holds more moisture and keeps the loaf soft for days.
sourceNotes: Shokupan (Japanese: 食パン, 'eating bread') is the standard Japanese white sandwich loaf, adapted from Western bread-baking traditions introduced to Japan in the nineteenth century. The tangzhong technique (a water roux or flour paste) was popularised in modern domestic baking by Yvonne Chen's 65°C Bread Doctor (2007) and has been widely reproduced in English-language baking guides. The basic tangzhong method and shokupan formula are fully public-domain.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice before freezing. Toasts from frozen beautifully.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best on day 2 after wrapping overnight. Stays soft for up to 4 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, 30 to 35 minutes until deep gold; internal temperature 88 to 90°C

glossaryTerms.definition: Tangzhong is a small portion of the dough's flour cooked with water or milk into a thick paste before being added to the main dough. At around 65°C, the starch in the flour gelatinises, allowing it to absorb and hold much more water than raw flour can. This extra moisture bakes into the loaf without making it dense. The result is a bread with a finer, softer crumb that stays fresh longer. To make tangzhong: whisk 30 g flour with 150 ml milk in a small saucepan and cook over medium heat, stirring, until the mixture thickens to a paste. Cool before adding to the dough.

content.text: Japanese milk bread is softer than almost any other loaf you can make at home. The tangzhong technique looks fussy but takes five minutes. It is what separates this loaf from a standard enriched white bread.
method. Whisk 30 g {{strong-bread-flour}} with 150 ml {{whole-milk}} in a small pan over medium heat, stirring until it thickens to a smooth paste. Cool to room temperature.
Make the dough, bulk ferment 90 minutes

Combine 320 g {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{yeast-dried}}, {{caster-sugar}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a large bowl or stand mixer. Add the cooled tangzhong, 100 ml warm {{whole-milk}}, and beaten {{eggs}}. Mix to a shaggy dough, then knead for 10 minutes by hand or 7 minutes in a stand mixer. Add the soft {{unsalted-butter}} in small pieces, continuing to knead until the dough is smooth and elastic. Shape into a ball, cover, and bulk ferment for 90 minutes until doubled.

Divide the dough into 4 equal pieces. Shape each into a round ball and place side by side in a greased 900 g loaf tin. Cover and proof for 60 minutes until the dough crowns above the rim. Preheat the oven to 180°C fan. Brush the top with any remaining beaten egg or milk. Bake for 30 to 35 minutes until deep gold. The internal temperature should reach 88 to 90°C. Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then turn out and cool completely.
